questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A horizontal circular loop carries a current that appears clockwise when observed from above. It is substituted by an equivalent magnetic dipole consisting of a south pole $S$ and a north pole $N$.
options: The line $SN$ must be perpendicular to the plane of the loop
The line $SN$ must lie along a diameter of the loop
The north pole must be positioned below the loop
The south pole must be positioned below the loop
questions.solution:
According to the right-hand thumb rule, for a circular loop carrying a clockwise current when viewed from above, the magnetic field lines pass through the loop from top to bottom.

This implies that the upper face of the loop acts as a South pole, as magnetic field lines enter it, and the lower face acts as a North pole, as magnetic field lines emerge from it.

When the loop is substituted by an equivalent magnetic dipole, the axis of the dipole, which is the line joining the South pole $S$ and the North pole $N$, must align with the magnetic field at the center of the loop. Thus, the line $SN$ must be perpendicular to the plane of the loop.

The magnetic dipole moment vector points from the South pole to the North pole. Since the magnetic field is directed downwards, the equivalent dipole must be oriented such that its South pole is above the loop and its North pole is below the loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For measuring the magnetic moment of a bar magnet, one could use
options: a tangent galvanometer
an oscillation magnetometer, provided the earth's horizontal field is known
a deflection galvanometer, provided the earth's horizontal field is known
both deflection and oscillation magnetometer, if the earth's horizontal field is not known
questions.solution:
The time period of oscillation of a bar magnet in an oscillation magnetometer is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{MB_H}}$. This gives the product $MB_H = \dfrac{4\pi^2 I}{T^2}$. If the earth's horizontal magnetic field $B_H$ and the moment of inertia $I$ are known, the magnetic moment $M$ can be determined.

In a deflection magnetometer, the deflection $\theta$ is related to $M$ and $B_H$ by $\dfrac{M}{B_H} = k \tan\theta$, where $k$ is a constant depending on the position (Tan A or Tan B). If $B_H$ is known, $M$ can be determined.

If $B_H$ is not known, we can use both instruments. The oscillation magnetometer gives the product $MB_H$, and the deflection magnetometer gives the ratio $\dfrac{M}{B_H}$. Multiplying these two quantities yields $M^2$, allowing us to find $M$ without knowing $B_H$.

A tangent galvanometer is primarily used to measure electric current, not the magnetic moment of a bar magnet.

questions.subject: Physics
options: A north pole corresponds to a clockwise current, and a south pole corresponds to an anticlockwise current.
Magnetic poles are merely mathematical assumptions that have no physical existence.
A bar magnet is equivalent to a long, straight current.
A magnetic field is produced solely by electric charges.
questions.solution:
Statement (1) is incorrect. According to the clock rule, an anticlockwise current acts as a North pole, and a clockwise current acts as a South pole.

Statement (2) is correct. Isolated magnetic poles (magnetic monopoles) do not exist in nature. They are merely mathematical assumptions used to simplify the description and calculation of magnetic fields.

Statement (3) is incorrect. A bar magnet is equivalent to a current-carrying solenoid, not a long, straight wire.

Statement (4) is incorrect. A magnetic field is produced by moving electric charges (currents) and changing electric fields, not by stationary electric charges.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic dipole is aligned in the north-south direction. Suppose $P_1$, $P_2$, $Q_1$, and $Q_2$ are four points placed at equal distances from the dipole towards the north, south, east, and west, respectively. The directions of the magnetic field due to the dipole are opposite at
questions.solution:
Let the magnetic dipole moment be $\vec{m}$ aligned along the North-South direction. Assume $\vec{m}$ points towards the North.

Points $P_1$ (North) and $P_2$ (South) lie on the axial line of the dipole. The magnetic field at any axial point is in the same direction as the dipole moment. Thus, the magnetic fields at $P_1$ and $P_2$ are both directed towards the North.

Points $Q_1$ (East) and $Q_2$ (West) lie on the equatorial line of the dipole. The magnetic field at any equatorial point is in the opposite direction to the dipole moment. Thus, the magnetic fields at $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are both directed towards the South.

Comparing the directions:
The magnetic field at $P_1$ is towards the North, and the magnetic field at $Q_1$ is towards the South. They are in opposite directions.
The magnetic field at $P_2$ is towards the North, and the magnetic field at $Q_2$ is towards the South. They are also in opposite directions.

Therefore, the directions of the magnetic field are opposite at $P_1$ and $Q_1$, and at $P_2$ and $Q_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic dipole is aligned in the north-south direction. Suppose $P_1$, $P_2$, $Q_1$, and $Q_2$ are four points placed at equal distances from the dipole towards the north, south, east, and west, respectively. The direction of the magnetic field generated by the dipole is identical at
questions.solution:
Let the magnetic dipole be placed at the origin with its dipole moment $\vec{M}$ directed along the North direction ($\hat{j}$).

Points $P_1$ and $P_2$ are located at equal distances $d$ towards North and South respectively. These points lie on the axial line of the dipole. The magnetic field at any point on the axial line is parallel to the dipole moment.

$\vec{B}_{P_1} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3} \hat{j}$

$\vec{B}_{P_2} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3} \hat{j}$

The magnetic field at $P_1$ and $P_2$ are identical in direction.

Points $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are located at equal distances $d$ towards East and West respectively. These points lie on the equatorial line of the dipole. The magnetic field at any point on the equatorial line is antiparallel to the dipole moment.

$\vec{B}_{Q_1} = -\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d^3} \hat{j}$

$\vec{B}_{Q_2} = -\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d^3} \hat{j}$

The magnetic field at $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are also identical in direction.
